subtopics: Atomic and molecular masses; isotopic abundance
Avogadro's number and the mole concept
Balancing chemical equations (by inspection and half-reaction)

glossary.definition: The SI unit for amount of substance, equal to 6.02214076e23 elementary entities (Avogadro's number).
The mass in grams of one mole of a substance, numerically equal to its formula mass in atomic mass units.
The reactant that is completely consumed first in a reaction, thereby determining the maximum yield of product.
The maximum mass of product predicted by stoichiometry from the limiting reagent.
The ratio of actual yield to theoretical yield expressed as a percentage.
The simplest whole-number ratio of atoms of each element in a compound.
The number of moles of solute dissolved per liter of solution.
Atoms of the same element with the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons, and hence different mass numbers.
A unit of mass equal to 1/12 the mass of a carbon-12 atom, approximately 1.66054e-27 kg.
The mass percentage of each element in a compound, calculated as (element mass / molar mass) x 100.
Amount of solute per unit volume of solution. Most common: molarity M = moles/liter.
Homogeneous mixture of solute dissolved in solvent. Properties: molarity, molality, mole fraction, percent composition by mass.
The smallest unit of an element retaining its chemical properties; consists of a nucleus (protons + neutrons) surrounded by electrons. Atomic number Z = proton count determines the element.
Two or more atoms covalently bonded together as the smallest unit of a chemical compound; characterized by molecular formula (atom counts) and molecular geometry.
A pure substance consisting of only one type of atom (same atomic number Z); cannot be broken down by chemical means; 118 elements in the periodic table.

To balance a redox equation, find the electrons lost/gained per atom from oxidation-state change, then scale each half-reaction so electrons cancel (their LCM). MnO4- -> Mn2+ is +7->+2 (gain 5 e-); I- -> IO3- is -1->+5 (lose 6 e-); LCM(5,6)=30 gives 6 MnO4- : 5 I-.

canon.name: Percent composition by mass
canon.form: % element = (mass of element in 1 mol compound / molar mass of compound) x 100%

subtopics: Ideal gas law and standard conditions
Real gases: van der Waals equation

glossary.definition: A hypothetical gas whose particles have no intermolecular forces and negligible volume, obeying PV = nRT exactly.
The pressure a gas would exert if it alone occupied the total volume at the same temperature.
The ratio of moles of one component to total moles in a mixture; chi_i = n_i / n_total.
The square root of the mean of the squared molecular speeds, equal to sqrt(3RT/M).
The passage of a gas through a tiny orifice into a vacuum; rate inversely proportional to sqrt(molar mass).
Empirical parameters a (accounts for intermolecular attractions) and b (accounts for molecular volume) in the van der Waals real-gas equation.
The ratio PV/(nRT); equals 1 for an ideal gas and deviates from 1 for real gases.
Defined by IUPAC as 0 degrees C (273.15 K) and 100 kPa; molar volume of ideal gas = 22.711 L/mol.
The average distance a gas molecule travels between successive collisions.
The statistical distribution of molecular speeds in an ideal gas at thermal equilibrium.

subtopics: First law: internal energy, heat, and work
Standard enthalpies of formation and combustion
Entropy and the second and third laws
Relationship between delta-G and the equilibrium constant
Temperature dependence of equilibrium (van't Hoff equation)
Chemical potential and partial molar quantities

glossary.definition: The total microscopic energy of a system; changes by dU = q + w (heat added plus work done on the system).
A state function defined as H = U + PV; at constant pressure, delta-H equals heat transferred.
A state function measuring the dispersal of energy; for a reversible process dS = dq_rev / T.
G = H - TS; a decrease in G at constant T and P indicates a spontaneous process.
A = U - TS; a decrease in A at constant T and V indicates spontaneity; equal to reversible work.
A reference condition: pure substance at 1 bar (solutes at 1 mol/L activity), specified temperature (usually 298.15 K).
The enthalpy change of a reaction equals the algebraic sum of enthalpies of any sequence of steps connecting reactants to products.
The amount of heat required to raise the temperature of a substance by one kelvin; C_P = (dH/dT)_P, C_V = (dU/dT)_V.
The partial molar Gibbs free energy of a species; mu_i = (dG/dn_i)_{T,P,n_j}.
The entropy of a perfect crystalline substance is zero at absolute zero temperature (0 K).
The enthalpy change of a process derived from the temperature dependence of the equilibrium constant via d(ln K)/d(1/T) = -delta_H/R.
The temperature dependence of reaction enthalpy: d(delta_H)/dT = delta_C_P.

canon.name: First law of thermodynamics
canon.form: dU = q + w  (or dU = delta_q - P_ext dV for PV work)

canon.name: Enthalpy definition
canon.form: H = U + PV  =>  dH = dU + P dV + V dP  =>  dH_P = q_P

canon.name: Gibbs free energy
canon.form: G = H - T*S  =>  dG = V dP - S dT

canon.name: Standard free energy and equilibrium constant
Fundamental thermodynamic relation
canon.form: dU = T dS - P dV + sum(mu_i dn_i)

canon.name: Kirchhoff's law
canon.form: delta_H(T2) = delta_H(T1) + integral(delta_C_P dT, T1, T2)

subtopics: Law of mass action and equilibrium expressions (K_c, K_p)
Reaction quotient Q and direction of reaction
ICE table method for equilibrium calculations
Solubility product K_sp and common-ion effect

glossary.definition: The ratio of product activities to reactant activities, each raised to stoichiometric coefficients, at equilibrium.
Equilibrium constant expressed in terms of molar concentrations.
Equilibrium constant expressed in terms of partial pressures (in bar or atm as specified).
The ratio of product to reactant concentrations (or pressures) at any point; if Q < K reaction proceeds forward.
A system at equilibrium responds to a stress by shifting in the direction that partially offsets that stress.
The equilibrium constant for the dissolution of a sparingly soluble ionic compound into its constituent ions.
The decrease in solubility of an ionic compound caused by the presence of a common ion already in solution.
An Initial-Change-Equilibrium table used to track concentration changes during an equilibrium calculation.
The fraction of a substance that has dissociated at equilibrium.
An equilibrium involving reactants or products in more than one phase; pure solids and liquids are omitted from the K expression.
The state of a reversible chemical reaction where the forward and reverse rates are equal, so concentrations remain constant; described by the equilibrium constant K.

canon.name: Law of mass action (generic)
canon.form: K_c = [C]^c [D]^d / ([A]^a [B]^b)  for aA + bB <=> cC + dD

canon.name: K_p from K_c
canon.form: K_p = K_c * (RT)^(delta_n_gas)  where delta_n_gas = moles gaseous products - moles gaseous reactants

canon.name: Reaction quotient criterion
canon.form: Q < K: forward; Q = K: equilibrium; Q > K: reverse

subtopics: pH, pOH, and the pH scale
Strong vs. weak acids and bases; K_a, K_b
Polyprotic acids and successive dissociation constants

glossary.definition: A species that donates a proton (H+) to another species.
A species that accepts a proton (H+) from another species.
A species that accepts an electron pair from a donor; broader than the Bronsted-Lowry definition.
Two species differing by a single proton: the acid donates H+ to give the conjugate base.
Acid dissociation constant: K_a = [A-][H+] / [HA] for weak acid HA.
Ion-product constant of water: K_w = [H+][OH-] = 1.01e-14 at 25 degrees C.
The negative base-10 logarithm of the hydrogen-ion activity (approximately [H+] in dilute aqueous solution).
A solution that resists changes in pH upon addition of small amounts of acid or base, typically a weak acid and its conjugate base.
pH = pK_a + log([A-]/[HA]); used to calculate pH of buffer solutions.
The point in a titration at which the moles of titrant added are stoichiometrically equal to the moles of analyte.
The point in a weak-acid titration at which [HA] = [A-] and therefore pH = pK_a.
The amount of acid or base a buffer can absorb per unit pH change; maximized when pH = pK_a.
A substance that donates protons (Bronsted-Lowry) or accepts electrons (Lewis). Strong acids fully dissociate in water (HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃). Weak acids partially dissociate (K_a < 1).
A substance that accepts protons (Bronsted-Lowry) or donates electrons (Lewis). Strong bases fully dissociate (NaOH, KOH). Weak bases partially ionize (K_b < 1).
Quantitative analysis by slowly adding a known-concentration solution (titrant) to determine the concentration of an unknown. Endpoint signaled by indicator color change.

canon.name: Relationship pK_a + pK_b
canon.form: pK_a + pK_b = pK_w = 14.00 at 25 degrees C (conjugate pair)

canon.name: Degree of dissociation for weak acid

subtopics: Integrated rate laws (zeroth, first, second order)
Activation energy and transition state theory (Eyring equation)
Reaction mechanisms and the rate-determining step

glossary.definition: An expression relating the rate of a reaction to the concentrations of reactants: rate = k[A]^m[B]^n.
The proportionality constant in the rate law; depends on temperature and the specific reaction but not on concentration.
The sum of the exponents in the rate law (overall order); the exponent for each reactant is the order in that reactant.
The time required for the concentration of a reactant to decrease to half its initial value.
The minimum energy that colliding molecules must possess for a reaction to occur; from the Arrhenius equation.
The frequency factor in the Arrhenius equation; related to the frequency of properly oriented collisions.
The highest-energy configuration along the reaction coordinate, corresponding to the top of the activation-energy barrier.
A single molecular event in a mechanism; its molecularity equals the order of that step.
The slowest step in a reaction mechanism, which limits the overall reaction rate.
A kinetic model for enzyme catalysis: v = V_max[S] / (K_M + [S]), where K_M is the Michaelis constant.
The assumption that the concentration of a reactive intermediate remains nearly constant throughout the reaction.

subtopics: Standard electrode potentials and the standard hydrogen electrode
Nernst equation and concentration dependence of EMF
Relationship between delta_G, E_cell, and K
Electrolytic cells; Faraday's laws of electrolysis

glossary.definition: A bookkeeping convention assigning hypothetical charge to an atom based on electronegativity rules in bonding.
The potential of a half-cell under standard conditions (1 M concentration, 1 bar partial pressure, 25 degrees C) relative to the SHE.
The reference half-cell with E^o defined as 0 V: H+(aq, 1M) + e- <=> 1/2 H2(g, 1 bar).
The electromotive force (cell potential) driving current in a galvanic cell; E_cell = E_cathode - E_anode.
Relates cell potential to the reaction quotient Q: E = E^o - (RT/nF) ln Q.
The charge of one mole of electrons, equal to 96485 C/mol.
The electrode at which oxidation occurs; in a galvanic cell it is the negative terminal.
The electrode at which reduction occurs; in a galvanic cell it is the positive terminal.
The additional voltage beyond the thermodynamic minimum required to drive an electrolytic reaction at a given rate.
A galvanic cell whose EMF arises solely from a difference in concentration of the same species at the two electrodes.
The loss of electrons by an atom/molecule in a redox reaction (increase in oxidation state); always paired with reduction (gain of electrons).
The gain of electrons by an atom/molecule in a redox reaction (decrease in oxidation state); occurs at the cathode in electrochemical cells.
An element characterized by metallic bonding, electrical conductivity, malleability, and tendency to lose electrons to form cations; located on the left/center of the periodic table.

canon.name: Gibbs free energy and cell potential
Equilibrium from E^o
canon.form: ln(K) = n*F*E^o / (R*T)  =>  log(K) = n*E^o / 0.05916 at 298 K

canon.name: Faraday's first law
canon.form: m = (M * I * t) / (n * F)  where m=mass deposited, M=molar mass, I=current, t=time

subtopics: Schrodinger equation; particle in a box
Hydrogen atom: quantum numbers (n, l, m_l, m_s) and orbitals
Radial and angular wavefunctions; probability densities
Aufbau principle, Hund's rule, Pauli exclusion principle
Periodic trends: atomic radius, ionization energy, electron affinity, electronegativity
Multielectron atoms: shielding and effective nuclear charge

glossary.definition: A mathematical function whose square |psi|^2 gives the probability density for finding a particle at a given point.
The principal quantum number (n = 1, 2, 3, ...); determines the energy and size of the orbital.
The azimuthal (angular momentum) quantum number (0 to n-1); determines orbital shape (s, p, d, f for l = 0, 1, 2, 3).
The magnetic quantum number (-l to +l); specifies orbital orientation in space.
The position and momentum of a particle cannot both be known exactly: delta_x * delta_p >= h/(4*pi).
The wavelength associated with a particle of momentum p: lambda = h/p.
Electrons fill orbitals in order of increasing energy, following the (n + l) rule.
Electrons occupy orbitals of equal energy singly with parallel spins before pairing.
No two electrons in an atom can have the same four quantum numbers; each orbital holds at most two electrons with opposite spin.
The net positive charge experienced by an electron after accounting for shielding by other electrons: Z_eff = Z - sigma.
The minimum energy needed to remove an electron from a gaseous atom or ion in its ground state.
The energy change when an electron is added to a neutral gaseous atom in its ground state.
A negatively charged elementary particle (charge −e = −1.6×10⁻¹⁹ C) surrounding the nucleus; its quantum states (orbitals) determine bonding and reactivity.
A mathematical function (wavefunction) describing the probability distribution of an electron in an atom; quantum numbers n, l, mₗ define each orbital (s, p, d, f types).
The arrangement of an atom's electrons among its orbitals (e.g. 1s2 2s2 2p6), filled according to the Aufbau principle, the Pauli exclusion principle, and Hund's rule.

canon.name: Schrodinger equation (time-independent)
canon.form: H_hat * psi = E * psi  where H_hat = -(h_bar^2/2m)*nabla^2 + V

canon.name: Particle in a box energy levels

subtopics: Lewis structures, formal charges, and resonance
Valence bond theory (hybridization: sp, sp2, sp3, sp3d, sp3d2)
Molecular orbital (MO) theory: bonding/antibonding MOs, bond order
MO diagrams for homodiatomic molecules (H2 through Ne2)
Intermolecular forces: London dispersion, dipole-dipole, hydrogen bonding

glossary.definition: In MO theory, (bonding electrons - antibonding electrons) / 2; higher bond order means shorter and stronger bond.
Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion theory; molecular geometry determined by minimizing repulsion between electron domains around the central atom.
The mixing of atomic orbitals to form new hybrid orbitals of equal energy directed toward bonding partners.
A measure of charge separation in a molecule: mu = q * d; units are debye (D) or C·m.
A strong electrostatic interaction between a hydrogen atom covalently bonded to a highly electronegative atom (N, O, F) and a lone pair on another such atom.
The energy released when gaseous ions come together to form an ionic crystal; related to ionic charges and radii via the Born-Lande equation.
A bookkeeping charge assigned to an atom in a Lewis structure: formal charge = (valence electrons) - (lone-pair electrons) - 0.5*(bonding electrons).
The description of a molecule or ion as a hybrid of two or more Lewis structures (resonance structures) that differ only in electron placement.
A measure of an atom's ability to attract bonding electrons to itself; increases across a period and decreases down a group (Pauling scale).
A transient induced-dipole/induced-dipole attraction present between all molecules; strength increases with polarizability and molecular mass.
Bond formed by sharing electron pairs between atoms. Lewis structure: shared pairs shown as lines. Polarity from electronegativity difference.
Electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions (cation+ and anion-). Forms between metals and nonmetals. Strong lattice energy.
Electrons in the outermost shell that participate in bonding. Determines bonding capacity; group 1A elements have 1 valence electron, etc.

canon.name: Dipole moment
canon.form: mu = q * d  (charge times separation distance)

canon.name: LCAO-MO (bonding/antibonding)
canon.form: psi_bonding = psi_A + psi_B;  psi_antibonding = psi_A - psi_B

subtopics: Stereochemistry: chirality, R/S, E/Z, diastereomers, meso compounds
Elimination reactions: E1 and E2; Zaitsev's rule
Electrophilic addition to alkenes; Markovnikov's rule
Electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS): nitration, halogenation, Friedel-Crafts
Nucleophilic addition to carbonyls (aldehydes, ketones, carboxylic acid derivatives)
Reactions of carboxylic acids and derivatives (esterification, saponification, amide formation)
Oxidation and reduction of organic functional groups

glossary.definition: An electron-pair donor that attacks electrophilic centers (electron-poor atoms); Lewis base.
An electron-pair acceptor that attacks nucleophilic centers; Lewis acid.
A species bearing a positively charged trivalent carbon; stability order: tertiary > secondary > primary > methyl.
A species bearing a negatively charged carbon; stability increased by adjacent electron-withdrawing groups.
Bimolecular nucleophilic substitution proceeding through a backside-attack transition state with inversion of configuration; rate = k[Nu][substrate].
Unimolecular nucleophilic substitution through a carbocation intermediate; rate depends only on substrate concentration; can give racemization.
In electrophilic addition to an unsymmetrical alkene, the electrophile (H+) adds to the carbon with more hydrogen atoms.
Elimination reactions preferentially give the more substituted (more stable) alkene product.
Groups that are related by symmetry operations: enantiotopic replacements give enantiomers; diastereotopic replacements give diastereomers.
Delocalization of electrons over multiple atoms, stabilizing a species relative to a localized structure.
A [4+2] cycloaddition between a conjugated diene (4 pi electrons) and a dienophile (2 pi electrons) to form a six-membered ring; syn-addition, endo rule.
An enol is a tautomer of a carbonyl compound with OH on a sp2 carbon; an enolate is its conjugate base, a resonance-stabilized carbanion.
Organic compounds contain carbon-hydrogen bonds (and typically C-C bonds); organic chemistry studies their structure, properties, reactions, and synthesis.

canon.name: Markovnikov's rule
canon.form: H adds to the less-substituted carbon (carbocation forms at more-substituted carbon)

canon.name: Diels-Alder [4+2] cycloaddition
canon.form: diene + dienophile -> cyclohexene  (syn addition, concerted, endo preference)

canon.name: Fischer esterification equilibrium
canon.form: R-COOH + R'OH <=(H+)=> R-COOR' + H2O  (K ~ 1, driven by removing H2O)

subtopics: Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR): chemical shift, coupling, splitting patterns
Mass spectrometry: molecular ion, fragmentation, base peak

glossary.definition: Absorbance A = epsilon * c * l, where epsilon is molar absorptivity, c is molar concentration, and l is path length.
The fraction of light that passes through a sample: T = I/I_0; A = -log10(T).
In NMR, the resonance frequency of a nucleus relative to a reference (TMS), expressed in parts per million (ppm).
The NMR coupling constant in Hz between neighboring nuclei, related to dihedral angle by the Karplus equation.
The intrinsic property of a substance relating absorbance to concentration and path length; units are L/(mol·cm).
The complex region of an IR spectrum from ~500–1500 cm-1 unique to each molecule; used for compound identification.
In mass spectrometry, the intact molecule that has lost one electron; its m/z equals the molecular weight.
The most abundant ion peak in a mass spectrum, set to 100% relative abundance.
The condition for constructive interference in X-ray diffraction: n*lambda = 2*d*sin(theta).
A quantum-mechanical rule specifying allowed transitions; for IR, a vibration must cause a change in dipole moment; for Raman, a change in polarizability.
Nuclear Magnetic Resonance spectroscopy; measures the magnetic environment of nuclei (¹H, ¹³C) to determine molecular structure; chemical shifts (in ppm) identify functional groups.
Study of matter-electromagnetic radiation interaction; types include IR (bond vibrations), UV-Vis (electronic transitions), NMR (nuclear spin), and mass spectrometry.

canon.name: Karplus equation (NMR coupling)
canon.form: J = A - B*cos(phi) + C*cos(2*phi)  where phi is dihedral angle

subtopics: Crystal field theory (CFT): splitting patterns for octahedral, tetrahedral, square-planar
Ligand field theory and molecular orbital treatment
Reaction mechanisms of coordination compounds (substitution, oxidative addition)
Organometallic chemistry: 18-electron rule, metal-CO and metal-alkyl bonds

glossary.definition: A compound containing a central metal atom or ion bonded to surrounding ligands via coordinate (dative) bonds.
An ion or molecule that donates electron pairs to a central metal atom; classified by denticity (mono-, bi-, polydentate).
The energy gap between the lower and higher d-orbital sets in a ligand field; Delta_oct > Delta_tet for same ligands.
Configuration of d electrons when Delta is less than or greater than the pairing energy, respectively; affects magnetic properties.
An ordering of ligands by their ability to split d orbitals: I- < Br- < Cl- < F- < OH- < H2O < NH3 < en < CO < CN-.
The enhanced thermodynamic stability of complexes formed by polydentate ligands compared to analogous monodentate ligand complexes, driven by entropy.
Stable transition-metal organometallic compounds tend to have 18 electrons in the metal valence shell (filled bonding MOs).
The energy gained by a complex due to preferential occupation of lower-energy d orbitals in a ligand field.
The ability of a ligand to labilize (facilitate substitution of) the ligand trans to it; strongest for high-field ligands (CO, CN-, H-).
A reaction in which a metal inserts into a bond (X-Y), increasing the metal's oxidation state by 2 and coordination number by 2.

canon.name: Crystal field d-orbital splitting (octahedral)
canon.form: e_g set (d_z2, d_x2-y2) raised by +3/5 Delta_oct; t_2g set (d_xy, d_xz, d_yz) lowered by -2/5 Delta_oct

canon.name: Crystal field splitting (tetrahedral)
canon.form: Delta_tet = (4/9) * Delta_oct (for same metal and ligands)

canon.name: CFSE (octahedral, weak field d^n)
canon.form: CFSE = (-2/5 * n_t2g + 3/5 * n_eg) * Delta_oct  (before pairing energy correction)

canon.name: 18-electron rule
canon.form: Total electron count = metal d electrons + electrons donated by all ligands = 18 for stable complex

canon.name: Stability constant (formation constant)
canon.form: K_f = [ML_n] / ([M][L]^n)  (product of stepwise formation constants K_1...K_n)

subtopics: Boltzmann distribution and the canonical ensemble
Partition function q: translational, rotational, vibrational, electronic contributions
Relationship between partition function and thermodynamic quantities (U, S, A, G, C_V)
Equipartition theorem and heat capacities of ideal gases
Molecular symmetry numbers in rotational partition functions
Absolute entropy calculation from spectroscopic data

glossary.definition: The sum over all quantum states of the Boltzmann factor: q = sum_i exp(-E_i / k_B T); encodes all thermodynamic information of a system.
The probability of a system being in state i: P_i = exp(-E_i / k_B T) / q.
A statistical mechanical ensemble of systems at fixed N, V, and T; the partition function is Q = q^N / N! for distinguishable or indistinguishable particles.

Each quadratic term in the classical Hamiltonian contributes (1/2)k_B T to the mean energy; translational and rotational modes each contribute (3/2)k_B T (linear), vibrational at high T contributes k_B T per mode.
Non-zero entropy remaining at 0 K due to degeneracy in the ground state (e.g., CO, ice); S_0 = k_B ln(W).
The number of equivalent orientations of a molecule obtained by rotations; used to avoid overcounting in the rotational partition function.
The energy (1/2)*h*nu_e of the lowest vibrational quantum level; non-zero even at absolute zero.
An ensemble of isolated systems at fixed N, V, and E; entropy is S = k_B ln(Omega) where Omega is the number of microstates.
An ensemble at fixed mu (chemical potential), V, and T; useful for open systems where particle number fluctuates.
A = -k_B T ln(Q); all other thermodynamic functions derived from A by standard relations.

canon.name: Molecular partition function
canon.form: q = sum_i g_i * exp(-E_i / (k_B*T))  where g_i is degeneracy of state i

canon.name: Internal energy from partition function
canon.form: U = k_B * T^2 * (d ln Q / dT)_V

canon.name: Translational partition function (3D)
canon.form: q_trans = (2*pi*m*k_B*T / h^2)^(3/2) * V  (de Broglie thermal volume)

canon.name: Rotational partition function (linear molecule, high T)
canon.form: q_rot = T / (sigma * Theta_rot)  where Theta_rot = h_bar^2 / (2*I*k_B)

canon.name: Vibrational partition function (harmonic)
canon.form: q_vib = exp(-Theta_vib/(2T)) / (1 - exp(-Theta_vib/T))  where Theta_vib = h*nu/k_B

subtopics: Variational principle and the variational theorem
Hartree-Fock method and self-consistent field (SCF) theory
Electron correlation: configuration interaction (CI), MP2, coupled cluster (CCSD(T))
Density functional theory (DFT): Hohenberg-Kohn theorems, Kohn-Sham equations
Molecular orbital symmetry and group theory in quantum chemistry
Potential energy surfaces and transition-state structure

glossary.definition: The assumption that electrons adjust instantaneously to nuclear positions because electrons are much lighter; separates nuclear and electronic wavefunctions.
The expectation value of the Hamiltonian for any trial wavefunction is greater than or equal to the true ground-state energy: <psi|H|psi> >= E_0.
A mean-field SCF method that approximates the many-electron wavefunction as a single Slater determinant of spin-orbitals; neglects electron correlation.
A finite set of mathematical functions (atomic orbitals) used to expand molecular orbitals in quantum chemistry calculations.
The difference between the exact non-relativistic energy and the Hartree-Fock energy: E_corr = E_exact - E_HF; always negative.
A method that replaces the many-electron wavefunction with the electron density rho(r) as the fundamental variable, based on the Hohenberg-Kohn theorems.
The unknown functional E_xc[rho] in DFT that accounts for exchange (Pauli) and correlation effects; approximated by LDA, GGA, hybrid functionals.
A high-accuracy post-HF method that expresses the wavefunction as exp(T)|HF>; CCSD(T) is considered the 'gold standard' for molecular energetics.
An antisymmetrized product of spin-orbitals that automatically satisfies the Pauli exclusion principle for fermions.
The electronic energy as a function of nuclear coordinates; stationary points correspond to equilibrium geometries and transition states.
A function psi whose squared magnitude |psi|^2 gives the probability density for a quantum system's configuration; it carries all measurable information and evolves by the Schrodinger equation.

canon.name: Hohenberg-Kohn theorem I
canon.form: The external potential V_ext(r) is determined uniquely (up to a constant) by the ground-state electron density rho(r).

canon.name: Kohn-Sham equations
canon.form: [-hbar^2/(2m) nabla^2 + V_KS(r)] phi_i(r) = epsilon_i phi_i(r)  where V_KS = V_ext + V_Hartree + V_xc

canon.name: Coupled cluster wavefunction ansatz
canon.form: |CC> = exp(T)|HF>  where T = T_1 + T_2 + ... (cluster operator)

subtopics: Amino acids: structure, pKa values, and classification
Protein structure: primary, secondary (alpha-helix, beta-sheet), tertiary, quaternary
Nucleotides and nucleic acids: DNA and RNA structure
Lipids: fatty acids, phospholipids, and membrane structure
Metabolic pathways: glycolysis, citric acid cycle, oxidative phosphorylation (overview)
Free energy changes in biological reactions (ATP hydrolysis coupling)
Henderson-Hasselbalch applied to biological buffers (phosphate, bicarbonate)

glossary.definition: A molecule containing an amino group (-NH2), a carboxyl group (-COOH), a hydrogen, and a side chain (R group) attached to the alpha carbon.
An amide bond formed between the alpha-carboxyl group of one amino acid and the alpha-amino group of another with loss of water; has partial double-bond character due to resonance.
The pH at which an amino acid (or protein) bears no net charge; pI = average of the pKa values flanking the zwitterion form.
The substrate concentration at which the reaction rate is half of V_max; reflects the affinity of the enzyme for its substrate (lower K_M = higher affinity).
Inhibition in which the inhibitor competes with the substrate for the enzyme active site; increases apparent K_M, V_max unchanged.
Regulation of an enzyme by binding of a molecule (effector) at a site other than the active site, inducing a conformational change that alters activity.
The primary energy currency of cells; hydrolysis of the terminal phosphoanhydride bond releases approximately -30.5 kJ/mol under standard biological conditions.
The anaerobic metabolic pathway that converts one glucose (6C) to two pyruvate (3C) molecules, producing a net of 2 ATP and 2 NADH.
The antiparallel, right-handed B-form double helix of DNA; adenine pairs with thymine (2 H-bonds), guanine pairs with cytosine (3 H-bonds).
The fundamental structure of biological membranes; amphipathic phospholipids arrange with hydrophilic headgroups facing aqueous phases and hydrophobic tails forming the interior.
A non-protein organic molecule that assists an enzyme; examples include NAD+, FAD, coenzyme A, and ATP.
The maximum velocity of an enzymatic reaction at saturating substrate concentration; equals k_cat times total enzyme concentration.

canon.name: Catalytic efficiency
canon.form: k_cat / K_M  (second-order rate constant for E + S -> product; diffusion limit ~ 10^8 to 10^9 M^-1 s^-1)

canon.name: ATP hydrolysis free energy (standard biological)
canon.form: delta_G^o' = -30.5 kJ/mol  (pH 7, 25 degrees C, 1 mM Mg2+)

canon.name: Glycolysis net reaction
canon.form: Glucose + 2 NAD+ + 2 ADP + 2 Pi -> 2 Pyruvate + 2 NADH + 2 ATP + 2 H2O

canon.name: Henderson-Hasselbalch (biological buffers)
canon.form: pH = pK_a + log([A-]/[HA])  (e.g. bicarbonate: pK_a1 = 6.1 at 37 degrees C)

subtopics: Precipitation titrations; Fajans and Mohr methods
Potentiometry: ion-selective electrodes, glass electrode, pH measurement
Chromatography: plate theory, resolution, van Deemter equation
Statistical treatment of analytical data: mean, standard deviation, confidence intervals
Propagation of error and significant figures
Calibration methods: external standard, standard addition, internal standard

glossary.definition: The effective concentration of a species accounting for non-ideal interactions: a_i = gamma_i * [i] / c^o (c^o = 1 M standard).
A dimensionless factor relating activity to molarity; approaches 1 at infinite dilution; decreases with increasing ionic strength.
A measure of the total ion concentration in solution: I = (1/2) * sum(c_i * z_i^2) for all ions i.
log(gamma) = -A * z^2 * sqrt(I) for dilute solutions; A = 0.509 in water at 25 degrees C.
An effective formation constant for a metal-EDTA complex at a specified pH, corrected for competing protonation equilibria of EDTA: K_f' = alpha_Y4- * K_f.
A measure of chromatographic column efficiency: N = (t_R / sigma)^2 or N = 16(t_R/W)^2.
A measure of chromatographic separation: R_s = 2(t_R2 - t_R1) / (W_1 + W_2); R_s >= 1.5 for baseline resolution.
H = A + B/u + C*u, where H is the height equivalent to a theoretical plate, u is the mobile-phase velocity, A=eddy diffusion, B=longitudinal diffusion, C=mass transfer.
A calibration technique for complex matrices in which known amounts of analyte are added to the sample solution to correct for matrix effects.

The method for calculating the uncertainty in a result derived from uncertain measurements; for sums/differences, absolute uncertainties add in quadrature; for products/quotients, relative uncertainties add in quadrature.

canon.name: Debye-Huckel limiting law
canon.form: log(gamma_i) = -A * z_i^2 * sqrt(I)  (A = 0.509 at 25 degrees C in H2O)

canon.name: Activity-concentration relationship
canon.form: a_i = gamma_i * c_i  (c in mol/L, relative to c^o = 1 M)
